[[RunningHead]]

Nine Lives: The Emotional Experience in General Practice. By Kenneth Sanders. Clunie Press for The Roland Harris Trust Library. £8. paperback. Pp. 156.

Andrew Elder

Andrew Elder

Andrew Elder  

This slim volume presents a GP-psychoanalyst's-eye view of a small number of general practice patients, seen over a longish period of time. It is unusual and well worth reading. It would be of particular interest to general practitioners with psychological or Balint interests, and other professionals working with, or alongside, general practitioners in the community.

Kenneth Sanders worked for almost thirty years as a general practitioner in North London, and at the same time developed an interest in psychoanalysis, later training to become an analyst. In this volume, and in his earlier book, A Matter of Interest, he calls on the primal experience of infancy and psychoanalytic theory, to help him understand and describe the patients seen in his surgery. He uses Bion's idea of a ‘proto-mental system’, in which the psyche and soma are inseparable, to illuminate many of the psychosomatic symptoms that form so much of a GP's work. His prose reflects this essential psychosomatic unity, easily moving from psychological to somatic imagery.

The heart of the book is the description of the Nine Lives of the title. Each one is an absorbing account of a small group of intertwining lives, observed over time (in some cases over twenty years) by their family doctor. The description of the doctor's interactions with his patients is set against a background of the changing social character of the North London suburbs between the 1950s and the 1980s. It is the Sanders's quality of human observation that makes the book special. He follows the strands of these patients’ lives in an unusually painstaking way, and with a great ear and eye for significant detail.

98

[[RunningHead]]

It is never the Right Time: How to Help a Mother to Separate from her Young Child

Maria Pozzi

Maria Pozzi

65 Holly Lodge Mansions, London N6 6HD.

Maria Pozzi  

In this paper I have described a short-psychotherapy treatment with a mother, a 3-year-old girl, and occasionally a father, over the difficulty in separating and settling down at nursery. The mother's unresolved mourning for the early miscarriage of her second baby was one important factor. She held on to the company and comfort provided by the little girl, who had become the vessel of maternal projections. Filled with Poppy's projections, the mother had also felt unable to withstand and process them. Mr Green was a conflictual element in the family, rather than a supportive husband and father, and Poppy's care and problems were left with the mother. Nine sessions unblocked the situation and allowed Poppy to settle down at a private nursery.

Our Responses to a Deadly Virus: The Group-Analytic Approach. By Angela Molnos. London: Karnac Books.

Derek Raffaelli

Derek Raffaelli

Derek Raffaelli  

This interesting book explores our reaction to one of the major challenges of our age: HIV/AIDS. In particular, Dr Molnos has set out to study the unconscious feelings stirred up by this internal destroyer and the psychological defences which are marshalled against such threatening anxiety. There is the minimum of discussion of medical and epidemiological data — just enough to remind us of the all-too-ignorable facts. The primary focus is on how the anxiety caused by this deadly virus can distort these facts and impact on our thinking and communications in prejudiced and confused ways.

responseSet.title: Mothering Psychoanalysis. By Janet Sayers. Harmondsworth: Penguin. Pp. 319. £6.99.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mothering Psychoanalysis. By Janet Sayers. Harmondsworth: Penguin. Pp. 319. £6.99.

Anne MacDonald

Anne MacDonald

Anne MacDonald  

Janet Sayers takes four female analysts, Helene Deutsch, Karen Horney, Anna Freud, and Melanie Klein, all of whom connect the early days of psychoanalysis with the present. She looks at their own lives and experiences as a means of enlivening and understanding their work. Focusing on each analyst in turn, Sayers starts with their personal and professional relationships with Sigmund Freud, and then helps the reader to understand their further journeys, separate but intertwining, that led psychoanalysis to a new consideration of the role of mothering, and towards its own continuing and diverse role in modern society. This is no mean task, as it would be only too easy to fall into a historical swamp, or captivate with tittle-tattle. In fact, she treats her subjects with respect and objectivity, looking carefully at their problems as well as their strengths, and is thus able to convey a great deal of their considerable respective achievements and theory in an informative and useful way.

‘There may be as many psychologies as there are psychologists,’ Sayers observes, and explains that she is looking at the very different experiences of being mothered and being ‘mother’ that shaped psychoanalysis. Thus the scene is set for an integrated view of these women, and the way in which they used their good and not-so-good experiences to help others.

This book is not an idealisation or celebration of mothering of the four key figures. It is an objective critique. I would recommend it to those who are new to the business of psychotherapy and psychoanalysis as providing not only a framework for the work of four very important people, but also the more recent work that has arisen from it. I recommend it also to more seasoned travellers in these realms, as an interesting and thoughtful book with an important theme. In my view it is well worth a read.

279

[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editor

Joscelyn Richards , Anne-Marie Sandler , Roger Kennedy , Sandy Bourne and Michael Göpfert

Michael Göpfert

Surrey, TW10 7JF.

Richmond, Surrey, TW10 7JF.
Park Avenue, Liverpool L18 8BU.

Michael Göpfert  

The British Confederation of Psychotherapists

We thought the readers of your Journal would be interested in receiving a brief report concerning the formation of The British Confederation of Psychotherapists, and an up-date of subsequent developments.

In January 1992, ten long-standing psychoanalytically-oriented organisations met informally to consider the developments taking place in the United Kingdom Standing Conference for Psychotherapy (now the UK Council for Psychotherapy). They were concerned that the diversity of standards and trainings, and the size of the Analytical Psychotherapy Section (now renamed the Psychoanalytic and Psychodynamic Psychotherapy Section), with its thirty-two organisations, posed insuperable problems to the establishment of appropriately rigorous standards for trainings at all levels.

As a result of this meeting, a Working Party was set up to make proposals for a new organisation that would adequately define and protect the training and practice of psychoanalytic psychotherapy, and on 8 March The British Confederation of Psychotherapists was formed. It has now been agreed that the character of the BCP is best represented by the following addition to the title: Psycho-Analysts, Analytical Psychologists, Psychoanalytic Psychotherapists and Child Psychotherapists. Together these disciplines comprise the field of psychoanalytic psychotherapy. The member organisations of the BCP recognise that there are other forms of psychotherapy, but consider they are competent to set standards only in relation to the training and practice of psychoanalytic psychotherapy.

The main aim of this organisation is to form a register of psychotherapists who have trained in psychoanalytic psychotherapy and who belong to an organisation whose standards of training and code of ethics are sufficiently rigorous to have been approved by the BCP's Registration and Ethics Committee.

The BCP has now completed its main documents. The constitution is currently being vetted by a lawyer; and when this process is completed the BCP would hope to be in a position to publish a register and have its documents available for those interested. It will then be in a position to respond formally to organisations interested in applying for membership.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Autistic States in Children. By Frances Tustin. Revised edition. Routledge. £14.99. Pp. 255.

Victor Sedlak

Victor Sedlak

Victor Sedlak  

This is an updated edition of Francis Tustin's 1981 book of the same title. The earlier book received great acclaim, not only from those professionals working in the field of child autism but from a wider audience of those concerned with the whole variety of mental health work from a psychoanalytic viewpoint. The author offered a theory of autistic functioning and its development which was convincing, extremely well described, and above all stemming from her humane wish to reach these extremely cut-off children.

In the present volume Tustin adds an introductory chapter, in which the clinical presentation of the autistic child is described. The other major change is a theoretical one. Tustin no longer believes that the origins of autism lie in a normal stage of development and stem from a regression to, or sticking in, that stage. She now regards that the autistic infant's failure to cathect the external world in preference for ‘swaddling’ itself in ‘swathes’ of auto-generated sensation to be pathological from the beginning. (I quote these words to give a flavour of Tustin's ability to convey the autistic child's experience with a poetic evocation.)

280

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Love and Death in the Transference: The Case of the Hungarian Poet Attila József

Mauro Mancia

Mauro Mancia

Via S. Marta 13, 20123 Milan.

Mauro Mancia  

This paper presents a selection of free associations in the form of blank verse, with their prologue, by the Hungarian poet Attila József who committed suicide in 1937 at the age of 32.

József's associations clearly reveal a negative transference towards his analyst, Edith Gyömroi, blamed for her inadequacy and inability to help him. This negative transference originated from his infantile resentment against an absent father and a mother who was psychologically dead, distant, unreliable, and treacherous, incapable of satisfying his need for love.

Attila's personality, characterized by elements pertaining to malignant and destructive narcissism, as Rosenfeld (1987) described it, reflects the infantile frustration and mental suffering brought about by his abandonment, and is dominated by narcissistic and substitute internal objects, like frail ‘prostheses’ defended by megalomanic phantasies and the omnipotence of thought.

[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Robin Anderson

Robin Anderson

Raleigh Park, Barnstaple Devon, EX31 4JB.

Robin Anderson  

With this issue (Volume 7 No. 3, 1993), my period as Editor of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy comes to an end. I feel very proud to have been given the opportunity to play a part in its development and growth.

When I look back to my Opening Editorial (Volume 3 Number 3, 1988), I notice that I was preoccupied with the context in which psychotherapy in the public sector was trying to establish itself; and I felt concerned at the cuts that were threatening some key services. From the perspective of where we are today, it is now clear that we were seeing the beginning of radical, far-reaching, and disturbing changes in the Health Service, which now, more than ever, put pressure on beleaguered psychotherapy services. At the same time, psychotherapy is more popular than ever before (despite the recent efforts of some journalists to discredit it), both with the public at large and with the medical profession. Psychoanalytic psychotherapy is more recognised in the Royal College of Psychiatrists and the other influential bodies. So that while all vulnerable services with long-term aims are under real threat, there is a sense of a greater public and professional awareness of why it is worth fighting for. While the pressures are in many ways greater, the morale is higher than I think it was in 1987.

I hope that by continued insistence on high-quality papers and an emphasis on good clinical work, Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy has played its part in this change, and I hope I can therefore feel I have been able to make a small, but important, contribution to psychotherapy in the public services, despite these troubled times.

Production of a Journal is a team effort, and I could not have achieved any-thing without the enthusiasm and support of the Editorial Board, past and present, David Bell, Marion Burgner, Denis Carpy, Peter Fonagy, Jane Milton, Bernard Roberts, Joan Schachter, Jon Sklar, Judith Trowell, Sally Weintrobe, and our editorial assistant, Ann Jameson. I would especially like to thank our contributors and our loyal readers, without whom there could be no journal.

responseSet.title: Who is the Mad Voice Inside?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Who is the Mad Voice Inside?

Michael Sinason BSC MBBS MRCPsych

Michael Sinason

Harlesden Road, London NW10 3RY.

Michael Sinason  

This paper will consider the identity of an inner voice that is experienced by everyone, but in very different ways. However, when working in the transference with psychotic patients the boundaries of personal identity and the question of choice and volition are so compromised by the illness that the phenomena are so to speak ‘writ large’. This allows the issues to be brought out with great vividness and intensity and it is the struggle with this that has led me to reconsider the identity of the inner voice in a way which I have found useful for the rest of my clinical work. Prior to 1985, my clinical work with patients with psychotic illnesses was undertaken in the in-patient psychotherapy ward at Shenley Hospital that I was responsible for. With the movement of psychotherapy resources out of Shenley to set up the Willesden Centre, our ability to continue with this work was very compromised because the Centre is entirely an out-patient resource. However, a number of us persevered and gradually worked out ways to be able to continue to offer treatment to patients who had psychotic illnesses. This involved the establishment of a case-manager system to enable the therapist to work in the transference. This evolution has occurred in a specialist workshop that I have been running at the Willesden Centre since 1986.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Independent Mind in British Psychoanalysis. By Eric Rayner. London: Free Association Books. £29.50 hardback £14.95 paperback. Pp. xii + 346.

Sandy Bourne

Sandy Bourne

Sandy Bourne  

This is a book of more than three hundred pages, twelve chapters, over a hundred sub-sections; and thirty-four pages of bibliography and thirty-three pages of index. It attempts a catalogue, synthesis, and exegesis of the work of the Independent Group of the British Psycho-Analytical Society, placing it in a historical and philosophical perspective. This is attempted within a structure of somewhat transcendental chapter headings (Emotion, Object and Person; Abstraction and Symbolisation; Creativity and Dreams; etc.) difficult to realise. The adopted method is a combination of essay, précis, and anthology of quotations; and it does become, too often, like struggling through a sentence with too many subordinate clauses.

Readers will look to this book to examine a coherent philosophy for the Independent group of psychoanalysts in Britain. Perhaps it is not enough simply to be psychoanalysts who abjure the other two groups. The characteristic identifying features are, of course, the stigmata of pedigree, genealogy; but it is useful to clarify what the family features are, as well as who was the mother or the uncle. In trying to do this, there are at least three agendas: firstly, to convey the universality of influences (and this comes near to writing a critical history of psychoanalysis); secondly, (and paradoxically) to define a characteristic and recognisable unifying voice; and, thirdly to make a thorough reconnaissance of the uncharted frontier with the Klein group. Independence seems intended to mean being open to influence from everywhere, free of dynastic chauvinism and group blindness; but, if we are looking for family features, it inevitably involves lineage and analytic family, just as it always has done in every analytic school. I felt that a prime factor in the rambling character of the book is some unacknowledged muddle over this. There is a magisterial sweep; but the over-inclusiveness, the cascade of names, quotations, and summaries is difficult, even where the material is familiar. This is certainly not a text for beginners who want to find out what's what.

From a Gang of Two Back to the Family**

Susan Kegerreis

Susan Kegerreis

67 Addison Gardens London W14 0DT

Susan Kegerreis  

This is a description of a somewhat unusual piece of work undertaken with two brothers who were seen together on a once-a-week basis over a period of three years. Seeing them together provided the opportunity not only to work on their relationship with the therapist in the transference, as it would have been done in individual therapy, but crucially to observe and work on their relationship with each other: seeing the use they made of each other in both encountering and avoiding their conflicts; as well as the dynamics, often of a ‘gang’ kind, in which they were caught as a defence against difficult feelings.

[[RunningHead]]

Mental Handicap and the Human Condition. New Approaches from the Tavistock. By Valerie Sinason. London: Free Association Books.

Joan Bicknell

Joan Bicknell

Joan Bicknell  

There was a time when people with learning difficulties (mental handicap) were put into institutions. It was assumed that if they could not think they could not feel. Their lack of a time concept guarded them against guilt about yesterday and worry about tomorrow.

In this book, the author, a principal psychotherapist from the Tavistock Centre, takes us on a very different journey and one that no-one has taken before. She shows through her work, teaching, and research that the lives of people with learning difficulties, however severe, and however hurt they are, can be transformed.

This needs new thinking and understanding by those around them based on traditional psychoanalytical concepts. For the lucky few, this help may come through psychotherapy treatment sessions, but for others there may be the chance to live in an environment, enriched by a greater understanding of their inner world and more sympathy for their psychological needs.

This book is a tour de force, encompassing case-histories that illustrate the need that some have to be ‘stupid’ to guard against further hurt. Self-injury, loss, sexuality, and, above all, abuse of all kinds, are addressed — from the therapy room, from history, and from the poets, of whom Sinason is one.

I read this book and I shall never be the same. I began to feel educated at last, validated in part, but above all hungry for more. This is more than a text-book, it is a personal experience shared unstintingly with the reader. You will not be disappointed.

97

This is the expanded and updated second edition of a well-known book which, to my shame, I had never before read. I had assumed, wrongly, that it was a guide or instruction manual for fledgling psychotherapists. In fact, it is aimed at a stage just before this, being ‘meant as a brief and simple introductory overview to the many forms of dynamic psychotherapy and their origins in and links with psychoanalysis.’

This modest statement of intent is easily fulfilled by the authors. Indeed, the overview provided by the book is impressive in its depth, as well as its breadth. The emphasis, however, remains on overview, and orientation to the context of psychodynamic theories and practices, both in a historical sense and in relation to the variety of approaches which are practised within modern mental health services. The reader is guided through this more general experience of orientation to a more specific understanding of the different approaches that could be described as psychodynamic.

A good deal of the book's content involves description of what is central to, and characteristic of, psychoanalytic therapy.

responseSet.title: The ‘Living Dead’ — Survivors of Torture and Psychosis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The ‘Living Dead’ — Survivors of Torture and Psychosis

Andrzej Werbart and Marika Lindbom-Jakobson

Marika Lindbom-Jakobson

Ektopsvägen 2 S-131 47 Nacka, Sweden.

Marika Lindbom-Jakobson  

Regardless of all differences between patients who have suffered from psychotic breakdowns and those with the sequelae of torture, our experience have shown that some common features can be recognized. We can attempt to understand patients’ experience of being ‘living dead’ in terms of regression to the ‘psychotic core’ in the personality, and in terms of actualisation and enactment of archaic relations to primary objects. The major difference between thee two categories of patients deals with a located, limited, and concrete traumatic event in the life of the torture-survivor, whilst in the schizophrenic individual the trauma also emanates from within through own destructiveness and rage. Regressive forms of relations toward distorted, aggressively cathected and persecutory primary objects are reestablished in both cases, leading to a more or less stable reorganisation of the ego. Difficulties in psychotherapy with schizophrenic patients and torture-survivors are here discussed as a consequence of the patients’ persistently holding onto the distorted and regressive forms of relating to primary objects, when the projections of the patients’ own destructiveness and hate is followed by a strong tendency to symbiotic merging.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Manic-Depressive Psychosis: Psychopathology and Individual Psychotherapy within a Psychodynamic Milieu

Murray Jackson

Murray Jackson

Le Pous, St André-de-Roquepertuis, 30630 France.

Murray Jackson  

Manic-depressive patients are not usually regarded by psychiatrists as suitable subjects for psychotherapy, and there are many reasons for this view. Psychological support tends to take the form of helping the patient adjust to his disability, to become more aware of the life situations which may disturb him, of warning symptoms, of the need for appropriate medication, and perhaps to explore the most significant life conflicts. Psychoanalytic psychotherapy aimed at conflict resolution and personality development is widely regarded as impracticable, or ineffectual at best, or, at worst, as a potentially dangerous intervention. Considering the fact that the risk of suicide in the depressive phases may be so great, the problems of containing the disinhibited manic episode so serious, and the social consequences of such episodes so disastrous, it is not surprising that pharmacological control of pathological symptoms is so often regarded as the only therapeutic aim.

This paper is based on an account of a single patient suffering from a severe manic-depressive disorder with schizoid features, and a family history of manic-depressive illness, who was treated with individual intensive psychoanalytic psychotherapy in a context of a psychodynamic psychiatric ward, with encouraging results. Some implications of this experience will be discussed.

responseSet.title: Cohabitation and the Negative Therapeutic Reaction
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Cohabitation and the Negative Therapeutic Reaction

Joscelyn Richards

Joscelyn Richards

Harlesden Road, London NW10 3RY.

Joscelyn Richards  

Borderline and psychotic patients especially, hut not exclusively, show genuine motivation towards achieving insight and change and then suddenly behave as if these have never been desired. When this shift occurs after a helpful interpretation it can be thought of as a negative therapeutic reaction. Patients can feel taken over by perceptions which drive them to think and behave in ways they later disagree with. These observations have led the author, along with colleagues at the Willesden Centre for Psychological Treatment, to recognise a situation of internal cohabitation of two autonomous minds in the one body which is a development of but not identical to Bion's concepts of psychotic and non-psychotic personalities. Some of the psychoanalytic literature on the different modes of mental functioning are reviewed and problems of appropriate conceptualisations are discussed. Clinical examples are presented to illustrate that differentiating the genuine negative therapeutic reaction from reactions to poor interpretations is facilitated and leads to further understanding of the patient's psychotic personality if the psychotherapist can hold in mind both the patient's and the therapist's psychotic and non-psychotic personalities.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Further Learning from the Patient. The Analytic Space and Process. By Patrick Casement. London: Tavistock/Routledge.

Nollaig Drake

Nollaig Drake

Nollaig Drake  

Patrick Casement clearly states his theoretical position at the outset. He is a member of the Independent Group of the British Psycho-Analytical Society, and, as such, he emphasises his freedom to draw upon whichever theoretical ideas seem to offer the greatest possibility of developing his clinical work with individual patients. He contrasts his approach rather pointedly with what he considers to be the more dogmatic approach of other schools of thought.

This volume is the sequel to Patrick Casement's first book, On Learning from the Patient, and is an elaboration of the themes introduced there, particularly the interactional processes between patient and analyst, and their evolution in each analysis. The author is especially interested in issues of technique, particularly from the patient's viewpoint. He believes that the analyst's sensitivity to the patient's response to interpretation can help in monitoring their impact, and that the patient can enable the analyst to formulate more effectively and specifically for him as a patient, thus minimising what Casement sees as the temptation to fall back on textbook analysis. He also emphasises the importance of creating and maintaining an analytic space within which the patient and the analyst can experiment. Careful monitoring of that space can alert us to impingements on it, and the task then becomes one of understanding the nature of the impingement and its significance for the patient.

This book is essentially an account of Patrick Casement's own work with his patients and supervisees. It is richly illustrated with clinical vignettes. His aim has been to reach a wider audience than he feels a theoretical book could. In this I think he succeeds. The book makes interesting, enjoyable, and informative reading. For those who may not have read the first volume, Chapter One of this book provides a comprehensive background to the influences and thinking which shaped the earlier work and which provide the starting-point for this sequel.

[[RunningHead]]

Audit in Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy with Children and Adolescents

M Thambirajah and Linda Winkley

Linda Winkley

Raddlebarn Road, Birmingham, B29 6JB

Linda Winkley  

The importance of multidisciplinary audit in child and adolescent psychiatry, including the work of child psychotherapists, is discussed. Research is highlighted which shows that certain groups of children in particular benefit from psychoanalytic psychotherapy. The problems related to auditing psychoanalytic psychotherapy are discussed, and a possible method of audit based on peer review of notes, which takes into account both inner and outer change, is presented.

A patient is discussed whose notes have been audited using the audit form. It is suggested that this form of audit has good educational potential as well as ensuring a high standard of patient care.

responseSet.title: The Revealing Image. Analytical Art Psychotherapy in Theory and Practice. By Joy Schaverien. London: Tavistock/Routledge.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Revealing Image. Analytical Art Psychotherapy in Theory and Practice. By Joy Schaverien. London: Tavistock/Routledge.

Alison Cookson

Alison Cookson

Alison Cookson  

To my knowledge, this book is the only published text which aims to explore the nature of the transference/countertransference relationships between art psychotherapist, the artist/patient, and the art work. The ways in which the picture influences the therapeutic relationship and itself becomes influenced by it, are addressed

193

from a number of different perspectives. Joy Schaverien argues that the picture itself can become a transference object and a means through which the subjective and objective nature of the patient's experience can come to be mediated.

Drawing predominantly on ideas from Jung, and the neo-Kantian philosopher Cassirer, Schaverien explores the processes involved in image-making and the processes which revolve around the image once it exists. In terms of the former, which Schaverien designates the life in the picture, she contrasts the differences between a diagrammatic image which may appear lifeless, as it visually describes and depicts something already consciously known, with an embodied image, which is one that can be achieved through the painter relinquishing the attempt to reproduce the picture seen in the mind's eye, and to allow the evolving images to interact with and inform the marks that its creator makes. The resulting embodied image, which captures and incorporates something of the essence of the painter's personal experience, is now contained within the boundaries of the picture and remains available, over time, for the many and various associations to it, to be explored within the therapeutic relationship.

The process of painting, with its shifts between primary identifications with the image, to more separate and objective perceptions of it may be echoed in the transference with the art psychotherapist. Working with the image, the art psychotherapist can explore these shifts as they become recreated in the psychotherapy and in what can become a highly charged triangular encounter. The way that meaning can be contained verbally and visually, and understandings gained using both these symbolic modes of expression, is followed through by Joy Schaverien in an original and stimulating way.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Lectures on Klein and Bion. Edited by Robin Anderson. New Library of Psychoanalysis No. 14. London: Routledge. Pp. 139. £9.99.

James Gallagher

James Gallagher

James Gallagher  

This book is based on nine public lectures presented in a series which aims to present developments in psychoanalysis to a non-specialist audience. The chapters are written by well-known and leading Kleinian analysts.

The first six chapters focus on certain of Klein's theoretical developments: the concept of unconscious phantasy, child analysis, projective identification, and the

279

paranoid-schizoid and depressive positions. There are a further three chapters focusing on Bion's development of Klein's ideas, in particular his work with borderline and psychotic patients, his development of Klein's ideas on projective identification, the idea of the container and the contained, and his theories on the development of the capacity for thinking.

In each chapter the author gives a concise description of the theoretical concept, describing the ways in which Klein's original ideas have been developed and modified by those coming after her, until the present day. As the title of the book suggests, however, this is not meant to be a dry, theoretical presentation. Each author illustrates the way in which these ideas have developed and how they are used in their clinical practice today. This brings the theories alive, and most of the clinical accounts are vividly described and interesting.

Although these lectures were originally aimed at a general audience, there is no suggestion of over-simplification in the presentation. The full complexity of the theories is described, and their continuing relevance in clinical work today is clearly demonstrated throughout.

The book is an enjoyable as well as an informative read. It will provide an excellent introduction to the work of Klein and Bion for someone coming to these ideas for the first time. It will also provide an opportunity for further thought and fuller understanding to those more experienced.

I would certainly recommend it.

280

Working with Psychotic Processes in Art Therapy

Katherine Killick

Katherine Killick

15 Kimberley Road St Albans Herts AL3 5PX

Katherine Killick  

In this paper the author describes in some detail her work in the art-therapy department of Hill End Hospital. She used not only her understanding of the work of psychoanalytical writers on schizophrenia, particularly Bion and Searles, but, more importantly, her own experience of the patients at the Hospital, to gain an insight into how these very regressed patients think of themselves and their relationship to reality. She illustrates how she used objects and images created by patients from the art materials supplied, during (usually) five two-hour sessions a week, together with conversations (and notes made in the patients’ presence of these conversations) about these images at structured weekly individual-therapy sessions, to help patients to develop from a state of meaninglessness to a position in which they can choose to engage in continuing therapy.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis, Psychiatry, Psychodynamics: Training for Integration**

Murray Jackson

Murray Jackson

Le Pous, 30630 St André-de-Roquepertuis, France

Murray Jackson  

Despite impressive advances in neurobiology and associated neuro-developmental theory, schizophrenia and related severe mental illnesses continue to follow a chronic or remitting course in the majority of cases.

Psychoanalytic knowledge provides an essential perspective in the attempt to understand and help the severely mentally ill, and such knowledge should not be incompatible with modern findings of neurobiology. The severely ill person is best treated from the outset in a hospital facility where his individual needs and capacities can be fully assessed within an integrated perspective, one which is able to provide a continuing resource as long after discharge as is necessary. A consistent and integrated philosophy of the nature of psychotic illness and its treatment can improve the quality of care provided by psychiatric wards, some of which are both inefficient and anti-therapeutic.

For such a gradual transformation to come about, training of all professional staffln integrating neurobiological and psychoanalytical knowledge is necessary. This paper presents some reflections on the subject deriving from the author's experience in the British National Health Service and in several Scandinavian countries.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Child of a Lesser God

Joan Hunter

Joan Hunter

25 Eton Rise Eton College Road London NW3 2DF

Joan Hunter  

This paper describes work with a man who was severely disabled physically. It taught me the profound emotional effects of such disability; but the effects of his feeling that he had not been loved because of his disability seemed even more important and far-reaching. Everything was much worse because of the development stage at which it had all occurred. It is important to make known the use which he made of analytical psychotherapy, for others like him could be helped.

I refer to some of the psychoanalytical literature which proved relevant to my work with this patient, give a report of his case, and describe the incidence of his sessions. After describing the beginning stage, I write selectively of work around the two main themes which can be identified: one, when he seemed locked in the closed system which constituted his inner world (Fairbairn 1958) from which he could gain relief only by sabotaging himself and the therapy; the other, when little by little the system began to open and gradually some investment in life began to emerge. There is then a brief account of the ending planned to a known date. The paper ends with some reflections on the work.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Price of Growing up

Grace Connolly

Grace Connolly

Slievemore Voltaire Road London SW4 6BZ

Grace Connolly  

This paper considers a seldom-discussed feature of assessment, namely, the significance of the setting of a fee and its connection with the setting in which a fee is agreed. Its importance is illustrated with reference to the three-times-weekly sessions over a period of one year with a single woman in her 30s. It will be seen how, in much the same way as aspects of her earliest history coloured her development from the start, the experience of setting the fee in the first session of treatment influenced subsequent features of the work.

In this case, the charging of a lowered fee will be shown to have affected the nature of the therapeutic relationship and it will be seen how the issue of payment of a fee at all provided a focus from which psychodynamically important material could be developed. It gave opportunity for the testing and working-through of a number of elements, both conscious and unconscious, throughout the first year of treatment. That is, the issue of the fee was found to be grossly over-determined — symptomatic of roots in several quite separate feelings and memories, each of which had to be uncovered before the issue could begin to be resolved.

Linguistic Style as an Indicator of Psychotherapeutic Regression: A Case Study

Rolf Sandell and Kerstin Lipschütz

Kerstin Lipschütz

S—118 52 Stockholm.

Kerstin Lipschütz  

‘Linguistic regression’ is proposed as a useful concept to study psychotherapy as a process of short-term regressions in the service of long-term progression. As an illustration of its potential, a case is presented in which stylistic variables showed a systematic change over time. Superimposed on a long-term trend towards increased control over linguistic regressions, brief interludes of linguistic regressions appeared to follow the therapist's addressing the patient's dependence conflict, particularly in the transference.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From fetus to child: an observational and psychoanalytic study. By Allesandra Piontelli. No. 15 in the New Library of Psychoanalysis ed. Spillius. Routledge. Pp 260.

Nollaig Drake

Nollaig Drake

Nollaig Drake  

In this book, Dr Piontelli has combined her considerable skills and multiple trainings in medicine, neuropsychiatry, child psychotherapy, and psychoanalysis to

283

produce a remarkable pioneering study. She uses ultrasonic scanning to observe eleven fetuses in utero, and follows their development after birth with the traditional method of infant observation developed by Esther Bick, but for an extended period of up to four years. In addition, Piontelli describes the psychoanalytic psychotherapy of one of these children, and the psychoanalysis of several other very young children whose behaviour gave strong indications of a continuing preoccupation with their prenatal experiences.

Although babies have always been readily available for observation in everyday life, it was mainly with the advent of psychoanalysis that we began to look with greater interest at infancy, and to formulate specific theories about infant development. A lively debate continues to unfold in relation to many of these theories, as new research findings emerge from different but related disciplines. Piontelli's detailed work extends the boundaries of this exploration and discussion, and is unique in the combination of observational methods it draws upon. She emphasises the importance of the researcher's state of mind in approaching new terrain, and characterises it as one of open-mindedness to whatever may emerge, a freedom from preconceptions, insofar as that is possible. It is in this spirit that Piontelli presents her findings, seeing them as a necessary preliminary observational phase, which could lead to the development of some tentative hypotheses requiring further investigation.

Piontelli convincingly demonstrates, through her detailed examples, that there is a subtle links of behavioural and psychological continuity extending from fetus to infant and from infant to child. Many interesting questions are raised about prenatal experience and its effect on postnatal development, some of which are already under consideration by the author herself in her subsequent work. In addition, the complementarity of the observational methods used in this study is implicit, and augurs well for some rich collaborative studies in the future.

responseSet.title: The Trauma of Attempted Suicide
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Trauma of Attempted Suicide

Eileen McGinley and Janet Rimmer

Janet Rimmer

Grafton Way LONDON WC1E 6AU

Janet Rimmer  

Suicide is a traumatic event, one which may acutely recreate a previously-experienced traumatic situation. The person's capacity to think after a failed suicide attempt is affected by the use of defence mechanisms against persecutory anxieties and depressive pain, and there is a wish to turn a blind eye to the seriousness of the suicidal act. This includes the damaging effects on the person's relationships with others, and damage to his own psychic integrity.

The need to turn a blind eye to the consequences of a failed suicide attempt may also be mirrored by the therapist who assesses and treats the patient in the early hours after such an event. Suicide is associated not only with the pain of despair but also with a ruthless abandonment of life. It is a complex and difficult task for a therapist in the immediate aftermath of a suicide attempt to be able to keep in mind a concept of suicide that includes both the patient's role as the perpetrator of great violence against himself, in which death is the intended outcome of his actions, and at the same time recognise the patient's identification with an abandoned and cruelly treated object.

In this paper, we describe some of the psychodynamic factors that affect and influence the assessment and treatment of patients in the immediate period following a failed suicide attempt. We emphasise the importance of supervision in helping prevent destructive acting-out by the therapist when treating disturbed patients under such painful circumstances.

On Separateness

Gabriele Pasquali

Gabriele Pasquali

Via Capo Pino 7, 16030 Sori (Genova), Italy.

Gabriele Pasquali  

In the author's view, closeness between patient and analyst enables the patient to gain insight into the pain of separation and thus to tolerate it, but at the same time brings up, paradoxically, a sense of separateness difficult to deal with. A distinction is made between separation and separateness.

Through clinical material, the author tries to show that while analysis can help patients to come to grips with the pain of separation, it has limited effect on the profound core of affects which have to do with fear of death and emptiness —the affects pertaining to separation.

responseSet.title: The Inner World and Joan Riviere. Collected Papers 1920-1958. Edited by Athol Hughes. Karnac. £18.95. Pp. 392.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Inner World and Joan Riviere. Collected Papers 1920-1958. Edited by Athol Hughes. Karnac. £18.95. Pp. 392.

Nollaig Drake

Nollaig Drake

Nollaig Drake  

Joan Riviere was a colleague, friend, and supporter of Melanie Klein, as well as being an analysand and renowned translator of Freud's writings. She was thus uniquely qualified for her task of showing the links between Freud's work and the discoveries being made by Klein. She is less well known for her own original contributions to the psychoanalytic literature.

This volume is an attempt to bridge that gap in our knowledge of her work. It is the first edition of Riviere's collected papers and it aims to present her own work in a systematic way. The book is organised partly on a chronological basis, and in part according to the subject matter. A glance at the table of contents allows us some insight into Riviere's key areas of interest and activity, and the reader will be struck by her expansive engagement with both Freud's and Klein's ideas and life, throughout her own personal and professional development

The book opens with a lively foreword by Hanna Segal, who was supervised in her analytic training by Joan Riviere. Segal gives a rich personal account of her contact with Riviere, whom she says ‘made the greatest impression on me in my formative years as a psychoanalyst.’ Segal concludes that Riviere was ‘an important original thinker’ and that ‘many psychoanalysts working today have no idea of the debt they owe to her contribution.’

Athol Hughes, who has edited this volume, introduces us to Riviere in a fascinating biographical essay. From this point onwards, the reader is drawn with interest and enthusiasm to follow the details of Riviere's papers as they unfold and to see the development in her thinking, and her capacity to integrate ideas.

The chapters which follow are each given a brief introduction by Hughes which very helpfully places the paper in the context, not only of Riviere's own development, but in the wider world of psychoanalytic thought. As such, the introductions provide the reader with a means of orienting himself, and they help to integrate what might otherwise seem like a mixed bag of papers.
